* **[Shoher](/wiki/Shoher "Shoher")** (שוחר), a person enrolled in pre-military studies (high school, technical college up to engineering degree, some of the קד"ץ courses) – after completing the twelfth study year will do a two-month boot-camp and, if allowed, enter a program of education to qualify as a [practical engineer](/wiki/Practical_engineer "Practical engineer"), with at least two weeks of training following each study year. Successful candidates will continue for an engineering [bachelor degree](/wiki/Bachelor_degree "Bachelor degree").
* **Civilian working for the IDF** ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): אזרח עובד צה"ל), a civilian working for the military.

The [Israeli Manpower Directorate](/wiki/Manpower_Directorate "Manpower Directorate") ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): אגף משאבי אנוש) at the [Israeli General Staff](/wiki/General_Staff_(Israel) "General Staff (Israel)") is the body which coordinates and assembles activities related to the control over human resources and its placement.

#### Regular service

Main article: [Conscription in Israel](/wiki/Conscription_in_Israel "Conscription in Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Trying_on_Uniforms_for_the_First_Time_%281%29.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Trying_on_Uniforms_for_the_First_Time_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Trying_on_Uniforms_for_the_First_Time_(1).jpg)

IDF conscripts trying on uniforms for the first time

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Nahal-kumta.jpg/250px-Nahal-kumta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nahal-kumta.jpg)

IDF [Nahal Brigade](/wiki/Nahal_Brigade "Nahal Brigade") soldiers on their regular service

National military service is mandatory for all [Israeli](/wiki/Israelis "Israelis") citizens over the age of 18, although [Arab](/wiki/Arab-Israeli "Arab-Israeli") (but not [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze")) citizens are exempted if they so please, and other exceptions may be made on religious, physical or psychological grounds (see [Profile 21](/wiki/Profile_21 "Profile 21")). The [Tal law](/wiki/Tal_law "Tal law") exempted ultra-Orthodox Jews from service. In June 2024, [Israel's Supreme Court](/wiki/Israel%27s_Supreme_Court "Israel's Supreme Court") unanimously ruled that Haredi Jews were eligible for compulsory service, ending nearly eight decades of exemption.[[37]](#cite_note-APNews20240625-37) The army began drafting Haredi men the following month.[[38]](#cite_note-TOI20240718-38)

Until the draft of July 2015, men served three years in the IDF. Men drafted since July 2015 serve two years and eight months (32 months), with some roles requiring an additional four months of Permanent service. Women serve two years. The IDF women who volunteer for several combat positions often serve for three years, due to the longer period of training. Women in other positions, such as programmers, who also require lengthy training time, may also serve three years.

Many [Religious Zionist](/wiki/Religious_Zionism "Religious Zionism") men (and many [Modern Orthodox](/wiki/Modern_Orthodox_Judaism "Modern Orthodox Judaism") who make [Aliyah](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah")) elect to do [Hesder](/wiki/Hesder "Hesder"), a five-year program envisioned by Rabbi [Yehuda Amital](/wiki/Yehuda_Amital "Yehuda Amital") which combines [Torah learning](/wiki/Torah_learning "Torah learning") and [military service](/wiki/Military_service "Military service").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Some distinguished soldiers are selected to be trained to eventually become members of [special forces units](/wiki/Sayeret "Sayeret"). Every brigade in the IDF has its special force branch.

Career soldiers are paid on average [NIS](/wiki/Israeli_new_shekel "Israeli new shekel") 23,000 a month. Conscripts are paid according to four tiers based on their positions. Frontline soldiers receive NIS 3,048 a month, other combat soldiers receive NIS 2,463, combat support soldiers receive NIS 1,793, and administrative soldiers receive NIS 1,235.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 1998–2000, only about 9% of those who [refused to serve in the Israeli military](/wiki/Refusal_to_serve_in_the_Israeli_military "Refusal to serve in the Israeli military") were granted an exemption.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In early 2026 it was made public that the IDF was considering expanding recruitment outreach to Jewish communities abroad in response to a manpower shortfall estimated at 10,000–12,000 soldiers.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Reports state that the initiative would target young diaspora Jews, encouraging them to immigrate to Israel and enlist, with a focus on communities in North America and Europe.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The move is described as a voluntary measure aimed at easing personnel shortages rather than altering Israel's core conscription system.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

#### Permanent service

Permanent service is designed for soldiers who choose to continue serving in the army after their regular service, for a short or long period, and in many cases making the military their career. Permanent service is based on a contractual agreement between the IDF and the permanent position holder.

#### Reserve service

Main article: [Reserve duty (Israel)](/wiki/Reserve_duty_(Israel) "Reserve duty (Israel)")

After personnel complete their regular service, they are either granted permanent exemption from military service or assigned a position in the reserve forces. No distinction is made between the assignment of men and women to reserve service.

The IDF may call up reservists for:

* reserve service of up to one month every three years, until the age of 40 (enlisted) or 45 (officers). Reservists may volunteer after this age, with the approval of the Manpower Directorate.
* immediate active duty in wartime.

All Israelis who served in the IDF and are under the age of 40, unless otherwise exempt, are eligible for reserve duty. Only those who completed at least 20 days of reserve duty within the past three years are considered active reservists.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Non-IDF service

Other than the civil, i.e. non-military "National Service" (*[Sherut Leumi](/wiki/Sherut_Leumi "Sherut Leumi")*), IDF conscripts may serve in bodies other than the IDF in several ways.

The combat option is [Israel Border Police](/wiki/Israel_Border_Police "Israel Border Police") (*Magav* – the exact translation from Hebrew means "border guard") service, part of the [Israel Police](/wiki/Israel_Police "Israel Police"). Some soldiers complete their IDF combat training and later undergo additional [counter terror](/wiki/Counter_terror "Counter terror") and Border Police training. These are assigned to Border Police units. The Border Police units fight side by side with the regular IDF combat units though to a lower capacity. They are also responsible for security in heavy urban areas such as [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and security and crime fighting in rural areas.

Non-combat services include the [Mandatrory Police Service](/w/index.php?title=Mandatrory_Police_Service&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mandatrory Police Service (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%A9%D7%99%D7%A8%D7%95%D7%AA_%D7%97%D7%95%D7%91%D7%94_%D7%91%D7%9E%D7%A9%D7%98%D7%A8%D7%94 "he:שירות חובה במשטרה")] (*Shaham*, שח"מ) program, where youth serve in the [Israeli Police](/wiki/Israeli_Police "Israeli Police"), [Israel Prison Service](/wiki/Israel_Prison_Service "Israel Prison Service"), or other wings of the [Israeli Security Forces](/wiki/Israeli_Security_Forces "Israeli Security Forces") instead of the regular army service.

### Women

Main article: [Women in the Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Women_in_the_Israel_Defense_Forces "Women in the Israel Defense Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Nightshot3332214.JPG/250px-Nightshot3332214.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nightshot3332214.JPG)

IDF Warrant Officers with the M16 and [IWI X95](/wiki/IWI_X95 "IWI X95"); two common assault rifles of the IDF.

Israel is one of only a few nations that conscript women or deploy them in combat roles, although in practice, women can avoid conscription through a religious exemption and over a third of Israeli women do so.[[47]](#cite_note-47) As of 2010, 88% of all roles in the IDF are open to female candidates, and women could be found in 69% of all IDF positions.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

According to the IDF, 535 female Israeli soldiers were killed during service in the period 1962–2016,[[49]](#cite_note-49) and dozens before then. The IDF says that fewer than 4 percent of women are in combat positions. Rather, they are concentrated in "combat-support" positions which command a lower compensation and status than combat positions.[[50]](#cite_note-Gaza:_It's_a_Man's_War-50)

Civilian pilot and aeronautical engineer Alice Miller successfully petitioned the High Court of Justice to take the Israeli Air Force pilot training exams, after being rejected on grounds of gender. Though president [Ezer Weizman](/wiki/Ezer_Weizman "Ezer Weizman"), a former IAF commander, told Miller that she would be better off staying home and darning socks, the court eventually ruled in 1996 that the IAF could not exclude qualified women from pilot training. Even though Miller would not pass the exams, the ruling was a watershed, opening doors for women in new IDF roles. Female legislators took advantage of the momentum to draft a bill allowing women to volunteer for any position if they could qualify.[[51]](#cite_note-feldinger-51)

In 2000, the Equality Amendment to the Military Service law stated that the right of women to serve in any role in the IDF is equal to the right of men.[[52]](#cite_note-mfaWomen-52) Women have served in the military since before the founding of the state of Israel in 1948.[[53]](#cite_note-womenIDF-53) Women started to enter combat support and light combat roles in a few areas, including the Artillery Corps, infantry units and armoured divisions. A few platoons named Karakal were formed for men and women to serve together in light infantry. By 2000, Karakal became a [full-fledged battalion](/wiki/Caracal_Battalion "Caracal Battalion"), with a second mixed-gender battalion, Lions of the Jordan (אריות הירדן, Arayot Ha-Yarden) formed in 2015. Many women also joined the [Border Police](/wiki/Israeli_Border_Police "Israeli Border Police").[[51]](#cite_note-feldinger-51)

In June 2011, Maj. General [Orna Barbivai](/wiki/Orna_Barbivai "Orna Barbivai") became the first female major general in the IDF, replacing the head of the directorate Maj. General Avi Zamir. Barbivai stated, "I am proud to be the first woman to become a major general and to be part of an organization in which equality is a central principle. Ninety percent of jobs in the IDF are open to women and I am sure that other women will continue to break down barriers."[[54]](#cite_note-The_Jerusalem_Post-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Yedioth_Ahronot-55)

In 2013, the IDF announced they would, for the first time, allow a transgender woman to serve in the army as a female soldier.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Elana Sztokman notes it would be "difficult to claim that women are equals in the IDF". "And tellingly, there is only one female general in the entire IDF," she adds.[[50]](#cite_note-Gaza:_It's_a_Man's_War-50) In 2012, religious soldiers claimed they were promised they would not have to listen to women sing or lecture, but IAF Chief Rabbi Moshe Raved resigned because male religious soldiers were being required to do so.[[57]](#cite_note-57) In January 2015, three women IDF singers performed in one of the IDF's units. The performance was first disrupted by fifteen religious soldiers, who left in protest and then the Master Sergeant forced the women to end the performance because it was disturbing the religious soldiers. An IDF spokesperson announced an investigation of the incident: "We are aware of the incident and already begun examining it. The exclusion of women is not consistent with the values of the IDF."[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Defense Minister [Moshe Ya'alon](/wiki/Moshe_Ya%27alon "Moshe Ya'alon") has also arranged for women to be excluded from recruitment centres catering to religious males.[[59]](#cite_note-59) As the IDF recruits more religious soldiers, the rights of male religious soldiers and women in the IDF come into conflict. Brig. Gen. Zeev Lehrer, who served on the chief of staff's panel of the integration of women, noted "There is a clear process of 'religionization' in the army, and the story of the women is a central piece of it. There are very strong pressures at work to halt the process of integrating women into the army, and they are coming from the direction of religion."[[60]](#cite_note-60)

[Sex segregation](/wiki/Sex_segregation "Sex segregation") is allowed in the IDF, which reached what it considers a "new milestone" in 2006, creating the first company of soldiers segregated in an all-female unit, the Nachshol (Hebrew for "giant wave") Reconnaissance Company. "We are the only unit in the world made up entirely of female combat soldiers," said Nachshol Company Commander Cpt. Dana Ben-Ezra. "Our effectiveness and the dividends we earn are the factors by which we are measured, not our gender."[[61]](#cite_note-61)

With the rise of social media platforms such as [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok") and [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), some critics claim that women in the IDF are frequently used as tools of propaganda, with official military accounts frequently posting attractive young women to create a sympathetic social media presence.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Minorities in the IDF

Non-Jewish minorities tended to serve in one of several special units: the [Sword Battalion](/wiki/Sword_Battalion "Sword Battalion"), also known as Unit 300 or the Minorities Unit, until it was disbanded in 2015;[[63]](#cite_note-63) the Druze Reconnaissance Unit; and the Trackers Unit, composed mostly of [Negev Bedouins](/wiki/Negev_Bedouins "Negev Bedouins"). In 1982, the IDF general staff decided to integrate the armed forces by opening up other units to minorities, while placing some Jewish conscripts in the Minorities Unit. Until 1988, the Intelligence Corps and the Air Force remained closed to minorities.

### Druze and Circassians

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/DruzeSoldier.jpg/250px-DruzeSoldier.jpg)](/wiki/File:DruzeSoldier.jpg)

A [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") commander of the IDF Herev battalion

Although Israel has a majority of Jewish soldiers, all citizens including large numbers of [Druze](/wiki/Israeli_Druze "Israeli Druze") and [Circassian](/wiki/Circassians_in_Israel "Circassians in Israel") men are subject to mandatory conscription.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Originally, they served in the framework of a special unit called "The Minorities' Unit", which operated until 2015 in the form of the independent Herev Gdud [("Sword") battalion](/wiki/IDF_Sword_Battalion "IDF Sword Battalion"). However, since the 1980s Druze soldiers have increasingly protested this practice, which they considered a means of segregating them and denying them access to elite units (like [sayeret](/wiki/Sayeret "Sayeret") units). The army has increasingly admitted Druze soldiers to regular combat units and promoted them to higher ranks from which they had been previously excluded.

In 2015, Rav Aluf [Gadi Eizenkot](/wiki/Gadi_Eizenkot "Gadi Eizenkot") ordered the unit's closure to assimilate the Druze soldiers no differently than Jewish soldiers, as part of an ongoing reorganization of the army. Several Druze officers reached ranks as high as Major General, and many received commendations for distinguished service. In proportion to their numbers, the Druze people achieve much higher—documented—levels in the Israeli army than other soldiers. Nevertheless, some Druze still charge that discrimination continues, such as exclusion from the [Air Force](/wiki/Israel_Air_Force "Israel Air Force"), although the official low-security classification for Druze has been abolished for some time. The first Druze aircraft navigator completed his training course in 2005. Like all Air Force pilots, his identity is not disclosed. During the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), many Druze who had initially sided with the Arabs deserted their ranks to either return to their villages or side with Israel in various capacities.[[65]](#cite_note-Gelber-65)

Since the late 1970s, the Druze Initiative Committee, centred at the village of [Beit Jan](/wiki/Beit_Jan "Beit Jan") and linked to [Maki](/wiki/Maki_(political_party) "Maki (political party)"), has campaigned to abolish Druze conscription.

Military service is a tradition among some of the Druze population, with most opposition in Druze communities of the [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights"). 83 per cent of Druze boys serve in the army, according to the IDF's statistics.[[66]](#cite_note-66) According to the Israeli army in 2010, 369 Druze soldiers had been killed in combat operations since 1948.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Bedouins and Israeli Arabs

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Bedouin_IDF1949.jpg/250px-Bedouin_IDF1949.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bedouin_IDF1949.jpg)

Bedouin soldiers in 1949

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Israeli_soldiers_and_Arabs.jpg/250px-Israeli_soldiers_and_Arabs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Israeli_soldiers_and_Arabs.jpg)

Israeli Arab soldiers, serving in the [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") in 1978

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Bedouin_Soldier_with_Schoolchild.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Bedouin_Soldier_with_Schoolchild.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Bedouin_Soldier_with_Schoolchild.jpg)

Bedouin Desert Reconnaissance Battalion, visiting an Arab school

By law, all Israeli citizens are subject to conscription. The Defense Minister has complete discretion to grant exemption to individual citizens or classes of citizens. A long-standing policy dating to Israel's early years extends an exemption to all other Israeli minorities (most notably [Israeli Arabs](/wiki/Israeli_Arab "Israeli Arab")). However, there is a long-standing government policy of encouraging [Bedouins](/wiki/Bedouin "Bedouin") to volunteer and of offering them various inducements, and in some impoverished Bedouin communities a military career seems one of the few means of (relative) social mobility available. Also, Muslims and Christians are accepted as volunteers, even if older than 18.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

From among non-Bedouin Arab citizens, the number of volunteers for military service—some [Christian Arabs](/wiki/Arab_Christians_and_Arabic-speaking_Christians "Arab Christians and Arabic-speaking Christians") and even a few Muslim Arabs—is minute, and the government makes no special effort to increase it. Six Israeli Arabs have received orders of distinction as a result of their military service; of them the most famous is a Bedouin officer, Lieutenant Colonel Abd el-Majid Hidr (also known as [Amos Yarkoni](/wiki/Amos_Yarkoni "Amos Yarkoni")), who received the Order of Distinction. Vahid el Huzil was the first Bedouin to be a battalion commander.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Until the second term of [Yitzhak Rabin](/wiki/Yitzhak_Rabin "Yitzhak Rabin") as Prime Minister (1992–1995), social benefits given to families in which at least one member (including a grandfather, uncle, or cousin) had served at some time in the armed forces were significantly higher than to "non-military" families, which was considered a means of blatant discrimination between Jews and Arabs. Rabin led the abolition of the measure, in the teeth of strong opposition from the Right. At present, the only official advantage of military service is the attaining of security clearance and serving in some types of government positions (in most cases, security-related), as well as some indirect benefits.

Rather than perform army service, Israeli Arab youths have the option to volunteer to [national service](/wiki/Sherut_Leumi "Sherut Leumi") and receive benefits similar to those received by discharged soldiers. The volunteers are generally allocated to Arab populations, where they assist with social and community matters. As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel_Defense_Forces&action=edit), 1,473 Arabs were volunteering for national service. According to sources in the national service administration, Arab leaders are counselling youths to refrain from performing services to the state. According to a National Service official, "For years the Arab leadership has demanded, justifiably, benefits for Arab youths similar to those received by discharged soldiers. Now, when this opportunity is available, it is precisely these leaders who reject the state's call to come and do the service, and receive these benefits."[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Although Arabs are not obliged to serve in the IDF, any Arab can volunteer. In 2008, a Muslim Arab woman was serving as a medic with unit 669.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Cpl. [Elinor Joseph](/wiki/Elinor_Joseph "Elinor Joseph") from [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa") became the first female Arab combat soldier for IDF.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Other Arab-Muslim officers who have served in the IDF are [Second Lieutenant](/wiki/Second_Lieutenant "Second Lieutenant") Hisham Abu Varia[[74]](#cite_note-ynetFirsftOff-74) and [Major](/wiki/Major_(rank) "Major (rank)") Ala Wahib, the highest ranking Muslim officer in the IDF in 2013.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

In October 2012, the IDF promoted Mona Abdo to become the first female Christian Arab to the rank of combat commander. Abdo had voluntarily enlisted in the IDF, which her family had encouraged, and transferred from the [Ordnance Corps](/wiki/Ordnance_Corps_(Israel) "Ordnance Corps (Israel)") to the [Caracal Battalion](/wiki/Caracal_Battalion "Caracal Battalion"), a mixed-gender unit with both Jewish and Arab soldiers.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

In 2014, an increase in Israeli Christian Arabs joining the army was reported.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Falasha_makstyle.jpg/250px-Falasha_makstyle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Falasha_makstyle.jpg)

An [Ethiopian-Jewish](/wiki/Beta_Israel "Beta Israel") soldier

Muslim Arabs have also been drafted into the Israel Defense Forces in increasing numbers in recent years. In 2020, 606 Muslim Arabs were drafted, compared to 489 in 2019 and 436 in 2018. More than half of those who have drafted have gone into combat roles.[[78]](#cite_note-jpost1-78)[[79]](#cite_note-greenger1-79)[[80]](#cite_note-motchan1-80)

### Ethiopian Jews

The IDF carried out extended missions in [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") and neighbouring states, whose purpose was to protect [Ethiopian Jews](/wiki/Ethiopian_Jews "Ethiopian Jews") (Beta Israel) and to help their immigration to Israel.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The IDF adopted policies and special activities for the absorption and integration of Ethiopian immigrant soldiers, reported to have much improved the achievements and integration of those soldiers in the army, and Israeli society in general.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) Statistical research showed that the Ethiopian soldiers are esteemed as excellent soldiers and many aspire to be recruited to combat units.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Haredim

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/NetzahYehudafield.jpg/250px-NetzahYehudafield.jpg)](/wiki/File:NetzahYehudafield.jpg)

IDF soldiers of the religious [97th "Netzah Yehuda" Infantry Battalion](/wiki/Netzah_Yehuda_Battalion "Netzah Yehuda Battalion")

Under a special arrangement called [Torato Umanuto](/wiki/Torato_Umanuto "Torato Umanuto"), [Haredi](/wiki/Haredi_Judaism "Haredi Judaism") men could choose to defer service while enrolled in *[yeshivot](/wiki/Yeshiva "Yeshiva")* and many avoided conscription altogether. This has given rise [to tensions](/wiki/Religious_relations_in_Israel "Religious relations in Israel") between the Israeli religious and secular communities. In 2024, the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Israel%27s_Supreme_Court "Israel's Supreme Court") unanimously ruled that Haredi men were eligible for compulsory service and the army began drafting them.[[37]](#cite_note-APNews20240625-37)[[38]](#cite_note-TOI20240718-38)

Haredi males have the option of serving in the [97th "Netzah Yehuda" Infantry Battalion](/wiki/Netzah_Yehuda_Battalion "Netzah Yehuda Battalion"). This unit is a standard IDF infantry battalion focused on the [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin") region. To facilitate Haredi soldiers to serve, the Netzah Yehuda military bases follow the standards of [Jewish dietary laws](/wiki/Jewish_dietary_laws "Jewish dietary laws"). The only women permitted on these bases were wives of soldiers and officers. Some Haredim serve in the IDF via the [Hesder](/wiki/Hesder "Hesder") system, principally designed for the [Religious Zionist](/wiki/Religious_Zionism "Religious Zionism") sector. It is a 5-year program which includes 2 years of religious studies, 1½ years of military service and 1½ years of religious studies during which the soldiers can be recalled to active duty at any moment. Haredi soldiers may join other units of the IDF but rarely do. Although the IDF claims it will not discriminate against women, it is offering Haredim "[women free](/wiki/Gender_apartheid#Judaism "Gender apartheid") and secular free" recruitment centres. Defense Minister Moshe Ya'alon expressed his willingness to relax regulations to meet the demands of ultra-Orthodox rabbis. Regulations regarding [gender equality](/wiki/Gender_equality "Gender equality") had already been relaxed so that Haredim could be assured that men would not receive physical exams from female medical staff.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

In 2024, the [Hasmonean Brigade](/wiki/Hasmonean_Brigade "Hasmonean Brigade"), a Haredi infantry unit, was created to accommodate Haredi soldiers. In January 2025, the unit received its first 50 soldiers.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Since the beginning of the [Israel-Hamas War](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"), 600 Haredim have enlisted through the [Shlav Bet](/wiki/Shlav_Bet "Shlav Bet") program, an IDF recruitment track intended for Haredim over the age of 26 to complete military service.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)

### LGBT people

Further information: [Sexual orientation and gender identity in the Israeli military](/wiki/Sexual_orientation_and_gender_identity_in_the_Israeli_military "Sexual orientation and gender identity in the Israeli military")

Further information: [Sexual orientation and military service § Israel](/wiki/Sexual_orientation_and_military_service#Israel "Sexual orientation and military service")

Since the early 1990s, sexual identity has presented no formal barrier in terms of soldiers' military specialization or eligibility for promotion.[[89]](#cite_note-eichner-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Until the 1980s, the IDF tended to discharge soldiers who were openly gay. In 1983, the IDF permitted homosexuals to serve but banned them from intelligence and top-secret positions. A decade later, professor Uzi Even,[[91]](#cite_note-barbara-91) an IDF reserves officer and chairman of [Tel Aviv University](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_University "Tel Aviv University")'s Chemistry Department, revealed that his rank had been revoked and that he had been barred from researching sensitive topics in military intelligence, solely because of his sexual orientation. His testimony to the [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") in 1993 raised a political storm, forcing the IDF to remove such restrictions against gays.[[89]](#cite_note-eichner-89)

The chief of staff's policy states that it is strictly forbidden to harm or hurt anyone's dignity or feeling based on their gender or sexual orientation in any way, including signs, slogans, pictures, poems, lectures, any means of guidance, propaganda, publishing, voicing, and utterance. Moreover, gays in the IDF have additional rights, such as the right to take a shower alone if they want to. According to a [University of California, Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara") study,[[91]](#cite_note-barbara-91) a brigadier general stated that Israelis show a "great tolerance" for gay soldiers. Consul [David Saranga](/wiki/David_Saranga "David Saranga") at the Israeli Consulate in New York, who was interviewed by the *St. Petersburg Times*, said, "It's a non-issue. You can be a very good officer, a creative one, a brave one, and be gay at the same time."[[89]](#cite_note-eichner-89)

A study published by the [Israel Gay Youth](/wiki/Israel_Gay_Youth "Israel Gay Youth") (IGY) Movement in January 2012 found that half of the homosexual soldiers who serve in the IDF suffer from violence and homophobia, although the head of the group said "I am happy to say that the intention among the top brass is to change that."[[92]](#cite_note-92)

### Deaf and hard-of-hearing people

Israel is the only country in the world that requires deaf and hard-of-hearing people to serve in the military.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Sign language interpreters are provided during training, and many of them serve in non-combat capacities such as mapping and office work. The major language spoken by the deaf and hard-of-hearing in Israel is [Israeli Sign Language](/wiki/Israeli_Sign_Language "Israeli Sign Language") (also called Shassi)–a language related to [German Sign Language](/wiki/German_Sign_Language "German Sign Language") but not Hebrew or any other local language–though Israel and Palestine are home to numerous sign languages spoken by various populations like Bedouins' [Al-Sayyid Bedouin Sign Language](/wiki/Al-Sayyid_Bedouin_Sign_Language "Al-Sayyid Bedouin Sign Language").

### Vegans

According to a [Care2](/wiki/Care2 "Care2") report, vegans in the IDF may refuse vaccination if they oppose animal testing.[[94]](#cite_note-C2-20121103-94) They are given artificial leather boots and a black fleece beret.[[95]](#cite_note-toi-vegans-95) Until 2014, vegan soldiers in the IDF received special allowances to buy their own food, when this policy was replaced with vegan food being provided in all bases, as well as vegan combat rations being offered to vegan combat soldiers.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

### Overseas volunteers

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Israel_Defense_Forces "Special:EditPage/Israel Defense Forces") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(March 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Non-immigrating foreign volunteers typically serve with the IDF in one of five ways:

* The [Mahal](/wiki/Mahal_(Israel) "Mahal (Israel)") program targets young non-Israeli Jews or Israeli citizens who grew up abroad (men younger than 24 and women younger than 21). The program consists typically of 18 months of IDF service, including a lengthy training for those in combat units or (for 18 months) one month of non-combat training and an additional two months of learning [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") after enlisting, if necessary. There are two additional subcategories of Mahal, both geared solely for religious men: Mahal [Nahal Haredi](/wiki/Netzah_Yehuda_Battalion "Netzah Yehuda Battalion") (18 months), and Mahal [Hesder](/wiki/Hesder "Hesder"), which combines yeshiva study of 5 months with IDF service of 16 months, for a total of 21 months. Similar IDF programs exist for Israeli overseas residents. To be accepted as a Mahal Volunteer, one must be of Jewish descent (at least one Jewish grandparent).
* [Sar-El](/wiki/Sar-El "Sar-El"), an organization subordinate to the Israeli [Logistics Corps](/wiki/Logistics_Corps "Logistics Corps"), provides a volunteer program for non-Israeli citizens who are 17 years or older (or 15 if accompanied by a parent). The program is also aimed at Israeli citizens, aged 30 years or older, living abroad who did not serve in the Israeli Army and who now wish to finalize their status with the military. The program usually consists of three weeks of volunteer service on different rear army bases, doing non-combative work.
* [Garin Tzabar](/wiki/Garin_Tzabar "Garin Tzabar") offers a program mainly for Israelis who emigrated with their parents to the United States at a young age. Although a basic knowledge of the Hebrew language is not mandatory, it is helpful. Of all the programs listed, only Garin Tzabar requires full-length service in the IDF. The program is set up in stages: first, the participants go through five seminars in their country of origin, then have an absorption period in Israel at a [kibbutz](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz"). Each delegation is adopted by a kibbutz in Israel and has living quarters designated for it. The delegation shares responsibilities in the kibbutz when on military leave. Participants start the program three months before being enlisted in the army at the beginning of August.
* [Marva](/wiki/Marva "Marva") is short-term basic training for two months.
* Lev LaChayal is a program based at [Yeshivat Lev Hatorah](/wiki/Yeshivat_Lev_Hatorah "Yeshivat Lev Hatorah") which takes a holistic approach to preparation for service. Being as ready as possible to integrate into Israeli culture, handling the physical challenges of the military, and maintaining religious values require a multi-pronged approach. The beit midrash learning, classes, physical training, and even recreational activities are designed to allow for maximum readiness.

## Doctrine

### IDF Code of Ethics

Main article: [IDF Code of Ethics](/wiki/IDF_Code_of_Ethics "IDF Code of Ethics")

In 1992, the IDF drafted a Code of Conduct that combines international law, Israeli law, Jewish heritage and the IDF's traditional ethical code—the [IDF Spirit](/wiki/IDF_Spirit "IDF Spirit") ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): רוח צה"ל, *Ru'ah Tzahal*).[[97]](#cite_note-idf-spirit-97)

The document defines four core values for all IDF soldiers to follow, including "defense of the state, its citizens and its residents", "love of the homeland and loyalty to the country", "human dignity" and "stateliness, as well as ten secondary values.[[97]](#cite_note-idf-spirit-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

"The Spirit of the IDF" (cf. supra) is still considered the only binding moral code that formally applies to the IDF troops. In 2009, [Amos Yadlin](/wiki/Amos_Yadlin "Amos Yadlin") (then head of [Military Intelligence](/wiki/Military_Intelligence "Military Intelligence")) suggested that the article he co-authored with [Asa Kasher](/wiki/Asa_Kasher "Asa Kasher") be ratified as a formal binding code, arguing that "the current code ['The Spirit of the IDF'] does not sufficiently address one of the army's most pressing challenges: [asymmetric warfare](/wiki/Asymmetric_warfare "Asymmetric warfare") against terrorist organizations that operate amid a civilian population".[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Details of the IDF's [rules of engagement](/wiki/Rules_of_engagement "Rules of engagement") remain classified.[[102]](#cite_note-nyt-rabin-24-102)

### Targeted killing

Main article: [Targeted killing by Israel](/wiki/Targeted_killing_by_Israel "Targeted killing by Israel")

Targeted killing, targeted prevention[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) or assassination[[105]](#cite_note-Gross-105) is a tactic that has been repeatedly used by the IDF and other Israeli organisations in the course of the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict"), the [Iran–Israel proxy conflict](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Israel_proxy_conflict "Iran–Israel proxy conflict") or other conflicts.[[105]](#cite_note-Gross-105)

In 2005, [Asa Kasher](/wiki/Asa_Kasher "Asa Kasher") and [Amos Yadlin](/wiki/Amos_Yadlin "Amos Yadlin") co-authored a noticed article published in the *Journal of Military Ethics* under the title: "Military Ethics of Fighting Terror: An Israeli Perspective". The article was meant as an "extension of the classical Just War Theory", and as a "[needed] third model" or missing paradigm besides which of "classical war (army) and law enforcement (police).", resulting in a "doctrine (...) on the background of the IDF fight against acts and activities of terror performed by Palestinian individuals and organizations."[[106]](#cite_note-106)

In this article, Kasher and Yadlin concluded that [targeted killings](/wiki/Targeted_killing "Targeted killing") of terrorists were justifiable, even at the cost of hitting nearby civilians. In a 2009 interview to *[Haaretz](/wiki/Haaretz "Haaretz")*, Asa Kasher later confirmed, pointing to the fact that in an area in which the IDF does not have effective security control (e.g., Gaza, vs. East-Jerusalem), soldiers' lives protection takes priority over avoiding injury to enemy civilians.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Some, along with [Avishai Margalit](/wiki/Avishai_Margalit "Avishai Margalit") and [Michael Walzer](/wiki/Michael_Walzer "Michael Walzer"), have disputed this argument, arguing that such a position was "contrary to centuries of theorizing about the morality of war as well as international humanitarian law",[[108]](#cite_note-108) since drawing "a sharp line between combatants and noncombatants" would be "the only morally relevant distinction that all those involved in a war can agree on."[[109]](#cite_note-109)

### Hannibal Directive

Main article: [Hannibal Directive](/wiki/Hannibal_Directive "Hannibal Directive")

The Hannibal Directive is a controversial procedure that the IDF has used to prevent the capture of Israeli soldiers by enemy forces. It was introduced in 1986, after some abductions of IDF soldiers in Lebanon and the subsequent controversial prisoner exchanges. The full text of the directive has never been published and until 2003 [Israeli military censorship](/wiki/Censorship_in_Israel "Censorship in Israel") even forbade any discussion of the subject in the press. The directive has been changed several times. At one time the formulation was that "the kidnapping must be stopped by all means, even at the price of striking and harming our own forces."[[110]](#cite_note-Weiz-110)

The Hannibal directive has, at times, apparently existed in two different versions, one top-secret written version, accessible only to the upper echelon of the IDF, and one "oral law" version for division commanders and lower levels. In the latter versions, "by all means" was often interpreted literally, as in "an IDF soldier was better dead than abducted". In 2011, IDF Chief of Staff [Benny Gantz](/wiki/Benny_Gantz "Benny Gantz") stated the directive does not permit the killing of IDF soldiers.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

### Dahiya doctrine

Main article: [Dahiya doctrine](/wiki/Dahiya_doctrine "Dahiya doctrine")

The Dahiya doctrine[[112]](#cite_note-112) is a military strategy of [asymmetric warfare](/wiki/Asymmetric_warfare "Asymmetric warfare"), outlined by former IDF Chief of General Staff [Gadi Eizenkot](/wiki/Gadi_Eizenkot "Gadi Eizenkot"), which encompasses the use of aerial and artillery fire against civilian infrastructure used by terrorist organizations[[113]](#cite_note-Byman-113) and endorses the employment of "disproportionate power" to secure that end.[[114]](#cite_note-harelEisenkot-114)[[115]](#cite_note-FinallyRealizes-115) The doctrine is named after the [Dahieh](/wiki/Dahieh "Dahieh") neighborhood of Beirut, where [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah") was headquartered during the [2006 Lebanon War](/wiki/2006_Lebanon_War "2006 Lebanon War"), which were heavily damaged by the IDF.[[113]](#cite_note-Byman-113)

## Budget

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(July 2024)* |

During 1950–66, Israel spent an average of 9% of its GDP on defense. Defence expenditures increased dramatically after both the 1967 and 1973 wars. They reached a high of about 30% of GDP in 1975, but have since come down significantly, following the signing of peace agreements with Jordan and Egypt.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

In September 2009, Defense Minister Ehud Barak, Finance Minister [Yuval Steinitz](/wiki/Yuval_Steinitz "Yuval Steinitz") and Prime Minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") endorsed an additional NIS 1.5 billion for the defense budget to help Israel address problems regarding Iran. The budget changes came two months after Israel had approved its two-year budget. The defence budget in 2009 stood at NIS 48.6 billion and NIS 53.2 billion for 2010 – the highest amount in Israel's history. The figure constituted 6.3% of expected gross domestic product and 15.1% of the overall budget, even before the planned NIS 1.5 billion addition.[[117]](#cite_note-haaretz.com-117)

In 2011, the prime minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") reversed course and moved to make significant cuts in the defence budget to pay for social programs.[[118]](#cite_note-118) The General Staff concluded that the proposed cuts endangered the battle readiness of the armed forces.[[119]](#cite_note-119) In 2012, Israel spent $15.2 billion on its armed forces, one of the highest ratios of defense spending to GDP among developed countries ($1,900 per person). However, Israel's spending per capita is below that of the US.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

## Equipment and weaponry

Main articles: [List of equipment of the Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/List_of_equipment_of_the_Israel_Defense_Forces "List of equipment of the Israel Defense Forces") and [Defense industry of Israel](/wiki/Defense_industry_of_Israel "Defense industry of Israel")

### Military equipment

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/IAF-F-35I-and-F-16I-nf.jpg/250px-IAF-F-35I-and-F-16I-nf.jpg)](/wiki/File:IAF-F-35I-and-F-16I-nf.jpg)

[Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") [F-16I](/wiki/F-16I "F-16I") and [F-35I](/wiki/F-35I "F-35I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Merkava-Tanks-Wikipedia-02.jpg/250px-Merkava-Tanks-Wikipedia-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Merkava-Tanks-Wikipedia-02.jpg)

[Merkava](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") ('Chariot') – Israeli main battle tank, with 4 generations

The IDF possesses various foreign and domestically produced weapons and computer systems. Some gear comes from the US (with some equipment modified for IDF use) such as the [M4A1](/wiki/M4A1 "M4A1") and [M16](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle") [assault rifles](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle"), the [M24 SWS](/wiki/M24_SWS "M24 SWS") 7.62 mm [bolt action](/wiki/Bolt_action "Bolt action") [sniper rifle](/wiki/Sniper_rifle "Sniper rifle"), the [SR-25](/wiki/SR-25 "SR-25") 7.62 mm semi-automatic sniper rifle, the [F-15 Eagle](/wiki/F-15_Eagle "F-15 Eagle") and [F-16 Fighting Falcon](/wiki/F-16_Fighting_Falcon "F-16 Fighting Falcon") [fighter jets](/wiki/Warplane "Warplane"), and the [AH-1 Cobra](/wiki/AH-1_Cobra "AH-1 Cobra") and [AH-64D Apache](/wiki/AH-64D_Apache "AH-64D Apache") [attack helicopters](/wiki/Attack_helicopter "Attack helicopter"). Israel has also developed its own independent weapons industry, which has developed weapons and vehicles such as the [Merkava](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") battle tank series, [Nesher](/wiki/IAI_Nesher "IAI Nesher") and [Kfir](/wiki/IAI_Kfir "IAI Kfir") [fighter aircraft](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft"), and various small arms such as the [Galil](/wiki/IMI_Galil "IMI Galil") and [Tavor](/wiki/Tavor "Tavor") [assault rifles](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle"), and the [Uzi](/wiki/Uzi "Uzi") [submachine gun](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"). Israel has also installed a variant of the [Samson RCWS](/wiki/Samson_RCWS "Samson RCWS"), a remote controlled weapons platform, which can include machine guns, grenade launchers, and anti-tank missiles on a remotely operated turret, in [pillboxes](/wiki/Bunker#Pillbox "Bunker") along the [Gaza–Israel barrier](/wiki/Gaza%E2%80%93Israel_barrier "Gaza–Israel barrier") intended to prevent [Palestinian militants](/wiki/Palestinian_militant "Palestinian militant") from entering its territory.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Israel has developed observation [balloons](/wiki/Military_ballooning "Military ballooning") equipped with sophisticated cameras and surveillance systems used to thwart terror attacks from Gaza.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The IDF also possesses advanced [combat engineering](/wiki/Combat_engineering "Combat engineering") equipment which includes the [IDF Caterpillar D9](/wiki/IDF_Caterpillar_D9 "IDF Caterpillar D9") [armoured bulldozer](/wiki/Armoured_bulldozer "Armoured bulldozer"), [IDF Puma](/wiki/IDF_Puma "IDF Puma") [CEV](/wiki/Combat_engineering_vehicle "Combat engineering vehicle"), Tzefa Shiryon and CARPET minefield breaching rockets, and a variety of [robots](/wiki/Robot "Robot") and explosive devices.

The IDF has several large internal [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") departments, and it purchases many technologies produced by the Israeli security industries including [IAI](/wiki/Israel_Aircraft_Industries "Israel Aircraft Industries"), [IMI](/wiki/Israel_Military_Industries "Israel Military Industries"), [Elbit Systems](/wiki/Elbit_Systems "Elbit Systems"), [Rafael](/wiki/Rafael_Advanced_Defense_Systems "Rafael Advanced Defense Systems"), and dozens of smaller firms. Many of these developments have been battle-tested in Israel's numerous military engagements, making the relationship mutually beneficial, the IDF getting tailor-made solutions and the industries a good reputation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In response to the price overruns on the US [Littoral Combat Ship](/wiki/Littoral_Combat_Ship "Littoral Combat Ship") program, Israel is considering producing their own [warships](/wiki/Warship "Warship"), which would take a decade[[124]](#cite_note-124) and depend on diverting US financing to the project.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### Main developments

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/INS_Lahav.jpg/250px-INS_Lahav.jpg)](/wiki/File:INS_Lahav.jpg)

[Israeli Navy](/wiki/Israeli_Navy "Israeli Navy") [Sa'ar 5-class corvette](/wiki/Sa%27ar_5-class_corvette "Sa'ar 5-class corvette") INS *Lahav*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/IDF-Ground-Forces-AFVs-01.jpg/250px-IDF-Ground-Forces-AFVs-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:IDF-Ground-Forces-AFVs-01.jpg)

IDF's current (2017) [armored fighting vehicles](/wiki/Armored_fighting_vehicle "Armored fighting vehicle"), clockwise: [IDF Namer](/wiki/IDF_Namer "IDF Namer"), [IDF Caterpillar D9](/wiki/IDF_Caterpillar_D9 "IDF Caterpillar D9"), [M270 MLRS](/wiki/M270_MLRS "M270 MLRS") and [Merkava Mk 4M](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava")

Israel's military technology is famous for its firearms,[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-:0-127) [armoured fighting vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicles "Armoured fighting vehicles") ([tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank"), tank-converted [armoured personnel carriers](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier") (APCs), [armoured bulldozers](/wiki/Armoured_bulldozer "Armoured bulldozer"), etc.),[[128]](#cite_note-128) [unmanned aerial vehicles](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned aerial vehicle"),[[129]](#cite_note-129) and rocketry (missiles and rockets).[[130]](#cite_note-130) Israel also has manufactured aircraft including the [Kfir](/wiki/IAI_Kfir "IAI Kfir") (reserve), [IAI Lavi](/wiki/IAI_Lavi "IAI Lavi") (cancelled), and the [IAI Phalcon](/wiki/IAI_Phalcon "IAI Phalcon") [Airborne early warning](/wiki/Airborne_early_warning "Airborne early warning") [System](/wiki/System "System"), and naval systems (patrol and missile ships). Much of the IDF's electronic systems (intelligence, communication, command and control, navigation etc.) are Israeli-developed, including many systems installed on foreign platforms (esp. aircraft, tanks and submarines), as are many of its [precision-guided munitions](/wiki/Precision-guided_munition "Precision-guided munition"). Israel is the world's largest exporter of [drones](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned aerial vehicle").[[131]](#cite_note-auto1-131)

[Israel Military Industries](/wiki/Israel_Military_Industries "Israel Military Industries") (IMI), as well as its former subsidiary [Israel Weapon Industries](/wiki/Israel_Weapon_Industries "Israel Weapon Industries"), are known for their firearms.[[127]](#cite_note-:0-127) The [IMI Galil](/wiki/IMI_Galil "IMI Galil"), the [Uzi](/wiki/Uzi "Uzi"), the [IMI Negev](/wiki/IMI_Negev "IMI Negev") light machine gun and the new [Tavor TAR-21](/wiki/Tavor_TAR-21 "Tavor TAR-21") Bullpup assault rifle are used by the IDF. The [Rafael Advanced Defense Systems](/wiki/Rafael_Advanced_Defense_Systems "Rafael Advanced Defense Systems") [Spike missile](/wiki/Spike_(missile) "Spike (missile)") is one of the most widely exported [ATGMs](/wiki/Anti-tank_missile "Anti-tank missile") in the world.[[132]](#cite_note-auto3-132)

Israel's [Arrow](/wiki/Arrow_(Israeli_missile) "Arrow (Israeli missile)") anti-ballistic missile system, jointly funded and produced by Israel and the United States, has successfully conducted combat intercepts of incoming ballistic missile attacks.[[133]](#cite_note-133) The [Iron Dome](/wiki/Iron_Dome "Iron Dome") system against short-range [rockets](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket") is operational and proved to be successful, intercepting hundreds of [Qassam](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket"), [122 mm Grad](/wiki/BM-21_Grad "BM-21 Grad") and [Fajr-5](/wiki/Fajr-5 "Fajr-5") artillery [rockets fire by Palestinian militants](/wiki/Palestinian_rocket_attacks_on_Israel "Palestinian rocket attacks on Israel") from the Gaza Strip.[[134]](#cite_note-Johnson-134)[[135]](#cite_note-Tory-135) [David's Sling](/wiki/David%27s_Sling "David's Sling"), an anti-missile system designed to counter [medium range rockets](/wiki/Medium-range_ballistic_missile "Medium-range ballistic missile"), became operational in 2017. Israel has also worked with the US on the development of a tactical high energy [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") system against medium-range rockets (called Nautilus or [THEL](/wiki/THEL "THEL")). [Iron Beam](/wiki/Iron_Beam "Iron Beam") is a short-range laser beam air defence system, created to eliminate rockets, artillery, and mortar bombs. With a range of several kilometres, it complements the Iron Dome system, which is specifically designed for intercepting missiles launched from greater distances.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

Israel has the independent capability of launching [reconnaissance satellites](/wiki/Reconnaissance_satellites "Reconnaissance satellites") into orbit, a capability shared with Russia, the United States, the United Kingdom, France, South Korea, Italy, Germany, the People's Republic of China, India, Japan, Brazil and Ukraine. Israeli security industries developed both the satellites ([Ofeq](/wiki/Ofeq "Ofeq")) and the launchers ([Shavit](/wiki/Shavit_2 "Shavit 2")).[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

Israel is known to have developed [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_and_Israel "Nuclear weapons and Israel").[[139]](#cite_note-fas-nuke-139) Israel [does not officially acknowledge](/wiki/Policy_of_deliberate_ambiguity "Policy of deliberate ambiguity") its nuclear weapons program. It is thought Israel possesses between one hundred and four hundred nuclear warheads.[[139]](#cite_note-fas-nuke-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) It is believed that [Jericho intercontinental ballistic missiles](/wiki/Jericho_(missile) "Jericho (missile)") are capable of delivering nuclear warheads with a superior degree of accuracy and a range of 11,500 km.[[141]](#cite_note-cns.miis.edu-141) Israeli [F-15I](/wiki/F-15I "F-15I") and [F-16](/wiki/F-16 "F-16") fighter-bomber aircraft also have been cited as possible nuclear delivery systems (these aircraft types are nuclear capable in the [US Air Force](/wiki/US_Air_Force "US Air Force")).[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-OTA-ISC-559-144) The [U.S. Air Force](/wiki/U.S._Air_Force "U.S. Air Force") [F-15E](/wiki/F-15E "F-15E") has [tactical nuclear weapon](/wiki/Tactical_nuclear_weapon "Tactical nuclear weapon") (B61 and B83 bombs) capability.[[145]](#cite_note-145) It has been asserted that [Dolphin-class submarines](/wiki/Dolphin-class_submarine "Dolphin-class submarine") have been adapted to carry [Popeye Turbo](/wiki/Popeye_Turbo "Popeye Turbo") [Submarine-launched cruise missiles](/wiki/Submarine-launched_cruise_missile "Submarine-launched cruise missile") with nuclear warheads, so as to give Israel a [second strike](/wiki/Second_strike "Second strike") capacity.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)

In 2006, Israel deployed the [Wolf Armoured Vehicle](/wiki/Wolf_Armoured_Vehicle "Wolf Armoured Vehicle") [APC](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier") for use in [urban warfare](/wiki/Urban_warfare "Urban warfare") and to protect [VIPs](/wiki/VIP "VIP").

### Field rations

[Field rations](/wiki/Field_ration "Field ration"), called *manot krav*, usually consist of canned [tuna](/wiki/Tuna "Tuna"), [sardines](/wiki/Sardines "Sardines"), [beans](/wiki/Beans "Beans"), [stuffed vine leaves](/wiki/Sarma_(food) "Sarma (food)"), [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") and [fruit cocktail](/wiki/Fruit_cocktail "Fruit cocktail") and bars of [halva](/wiki/Halva "Halva"). Packets of fruit-flavoured drink powder are provided along with condiments like [ketchup](/wiki/Ketchup "Ketchup"), [mustard](/wiki/Mustard_(condiment) "Mustard (condiment)"), chocolate spread and jam. Around 2010, the IDF announced that certain freeze-dried [MREs](/wiki/MRE "MRE") served in water-activated disposable heaters like [goulash](/wiki/Goulash "Goulash"), turkey [schwarma](/wiki/Schwarma "Schwarma") and [meatballs](/wiki/Meatballs "Meatballs") would be introduced as field rations.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

One staple of these rations was *[loof](/wiki/Spam_(food)#Israel "Spam (food)")*, a type of Kosher [spam](/wiki/Spam_(food) "Spam (food)") made from chicken or beef that was phased out around 2008.[[149]](#cite_note-149) Food historian Gil Marks has written that: "Many Israeli soldiers insist that Loof uses all the parts of the cow that the hot dog manufacturers will not accept, but no one outside of the manufacturer and the kosher supervisors know what is inside."[[150]](#cite_note-150)

### Development plans

See also: [Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II Israeli procurement](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_F-35_Lightning_II_Israeli_procurement "Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II Israeli procurement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Merkeva_Mk_4M_-_Israeli_Tanks_2019-04-21_IZE-42.jpg/250px-Merkeva_Mk_4M_-_Israeli_Tanks_2019-04-21_IZE-42.jpg)](/wiki/File:Merkeva_Mk_4M_-_Israeli_Tanks_2019-04-21_IZE-42.jpg)

A profile of a Merkava Mk 4M tank, armed with an [IMI 120 mm gun](/wiki/IMI_120_mm_gun "IMI 120 mm gun"), a [M2 Browning](/wiki/M2_Browning "M2 Browning") .50-cal, a [7.62x51 mm NATO](/wiki/7.62x51_mm_NATO "7.62x51 mm NATO") commander's [FN MAG](/wiki/FN_MAG "FN MAG"), and equipped with the [Trophy active protection system](/wiki/Trophy_(countermeasure) "Trophy (countermeasure)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/IAF-F-35I-2016-12-13_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-IAF-F-35I-2016-12-13_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:IAF-F-35I-2016-12-13_(cropped).jpg)

[Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") [F-35I Adir](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_F-35_Lightning_II_Israeli_procurement "Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II Israeli procurement")

The IDF is planning several technological upgrades and structural reforms for the future of its land, air, and sea branches. Training has been increased, including cooperation between ground, air, and naval units.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

The Israeli Army is phasing out the M-16 rifle from all ground units in favor of the [IMI Tavor](/wiki/IMI_Tavor "IMI Tavor") variants, most recently the [IWI Tavor X95 flat-top](/wiki/IWI_X95 "IWI X95") ("Micro-Tavor Dor Gimel").[[152]](#cite_note-152) The IDF is replacing its outdated [M113 armored personnel carriers](/wiki/M113_armored_personnel_carrier "M113 armored personnel carrier") in favor of new [Namer](/wiki/Namer "Namer") APCs, with 200 ordered in 2014, the [Eitan AFV](/wiki/Eitan_AFV "Eitan AFV"), and is upgrading its [IDF Achzarit](/wiki/IDF_Achzarit "IDF Achzarit") APCs.[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154) The IDF announced plans to streamline its military bureaucracy so as to better maintain its reserve force, which a 2014 State Comptroller report noted was under-trained and may not be able to fulfill wartime missions. As part of the plans, 100,000 reservists will be discharged, and training for the remainder will be improved. The officer corps will be slashed by 5,000. Infantry and light artillery brigades will be reduced to increase training standards among the rest.[[155]](#cite_note-155)

The IDF is planning a future tank to replace the Merkava. The new tank will be able to fire lasers and electromagnetic pulses, run on a hybrid engine, run with a crew as small as two, will be faster, and will be better protected, with emphasis on protection systems such as the Trophy over armour.[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157) The [Combat Engineering Corps](/wiki/Combat_Engineering_Corps "Combat Engineering Corps") assimilated new technologies, mainly in tunnel detection and [unmanned ground vehicles](/wiki/Unmanned_ground_vehicle "Unmanned ground vehicle") and [military robots](/wiki/Military_robot "Military robot"), such as remote-controlled [IDF Caterpillar D9](/wiki/IDF_Caterpillar_D9 "IDF Caterpillar D9")T "Panda" armoured bulldozers, Sahar engineering scout robot and improved [Remotec ANDROS](/wiki/Remotec_ANDROS "Remotec ANDROS") robots.

The [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") will purchase as many as 100 [F-35 Lightning II](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_F-35_Lightning_II "Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II") fighter jets from the United States. The aircraft will be modified and designated F-35I. They will use Israeli-built electronic warfare systems, outer wings, guided bombs, and air-to-air missiles.[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160) As part of a 2013 arms deal, the IAF will purchase [KC-135 Stratotanker](/wiki/Boeing_KC-135_Stratotanker "Boeing KC-135 Stratotanker") aerial refueling aircraft and [V-22 Osprey](/wiki/Bell_Boeing_V-22_Osprey "Bell Boeing V-22 Osprey") multi-mission aircraft from the United States, as well as advanced radars for warplanes and missiles designed to take out radars.[[161]](#cite_note-161) In April 2013, an Israeli official stated that within 40–50 years, piloted aircraft would be phased out of service by [unmanned aerial vehicles](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicles "Unmanned aerial vehicles") capable of executing nearly any operation that can be performed by piloted combat aircraft. Israel's military industries are reportedly on the path to developing such technology in a few decades. Israel will also manufacture tactical satellites for military use.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

The [Israeli Navy](/wiki/Israeli_Navy "Israeli Navy") is currently expanding its submarine fleet, with a planned total of six [Dolphin class submarines](/wiki/Dolphin_class_submarine "Dolphin class submarine"). Currently, five have been delivered, with the sixth, INS Drakon, expected to be delivered in 2020.[[163]](#cite_note-163) It is also upgrading and expanding its surface fleet. It is planning to upgrade the electronic warfare systems of its [Sa'ar 5-class corvettes](/wiki/Sa%27ar_5-class_corvette "Sa'ar 5-class corvette") and [Sa'ar 4.5 class missile boats](/wiki/Sa%27ar_4.5_class_missile_boat "Sa'ar 4.5 class missile boat"),[[164]](#cite_note-164) and has ordered two new classes of warship: the [Sa'ar 6-class corvette](/wiki/Sa%27ar_6-class_corvette "Sa'ar 6-class corvette") (a variant of the [Braunschweig-class corvette](/wiki/Braunschweig-class_corvette "Braunschweig-class corvette")) and the [Sa'ar 72-class corvette](/wiki/Sa%27ar_72-class_corvette "Sa'ar 72-class corvette"), an improved and enlarged version of the Sa'ar 4.5-class. It plans to acquire four Saar 6-class corvettes and three Sa'ar 72-class corvettes. Israel is also developing marine artillery, including a gun capable of firing satellite-guided 155mm rounds between 75 and 120 kilometres.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

## Foreign military relations

### France

Further information: [France–Israel relations](/wiki/France%E2%80%93Israel_relations "France–Israel relations")

Starting on 14 May 1948 (5 Iyar 5708), when Israel became a sovereign state, a strong military, commercial and political relationship was established between France and Israel, which lasted until 1969. Between 1956 and 1966, the two countries had the highest level of military collaboration.[[166]](#cite_note-166)

### United States

Main article: [Israel–United States military relations](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_military_relations "Israel–United States military relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Gen._Martin_E._Dempsey_meets_with_Lt._Gen._Benny_Gantz.jpg/250px-Gen._Martin_E._Dempsey_meets_with_Lt._Gen._Benny_Gantz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gen._Martin_E._Dempsey_meets_with_Lt._Gen._Benny_Gantz.jpg)

Former IDF Chief of Staff Benny Gantz (right) meets with [Martin Dempsey](/wiki/Martin_Dempsey "Martin Dempsey") (left), Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/US_Navy_050415-M-9114Y-008_Marines_assigned_to_the_26th_Marine_Expeditionary_Unit_%28special_operations_capable%29_along_Israeli_Defense_Force_soldiers_fast_rope_from_a_CH-46E_Sea_Knight_helicopter.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_050415-M-9114Y-008_Marines_assigned_to_the_26th_Marine_Expeditionary_Unit_(special_operations_capable)_along_Israeli_Defense_Force_soldiers_fast_rope_from_a_CH-46E_Sea_Knight_helicopter.jpg)

Israeli soldiers training alongside the [26th Marine Expeditionary Unit](/wiki/26th_Marine_Expeditionary_Unit "26th Marine Expeditionary Unit") on the [USS *Kearsarge*](/wiki/USS_Kearsarge_(LHD-3) "USS Kearsarge (LHD-3)")

In 1983, the United States and Israel established a [Joint Political Military Group](/wiki/Joint_Political_Military_Group "Joint Political Military Group"), which convenes twice a year. Both the U.S. and Israel participate in joint military planning and combined exercises and have collaborated on military research and weapons development. Additionally the [U.S. military](/wiki/Military_of_the_United_States "Military of the United States") maintains two classified, pre-positioned [War Reserve Stocks](/wiki/War_Reserve_Stock "War Reserve Stock") in Israel, which were estimated to have contained up to $4.4 billion worth of weaponry prior to [the attacks](/wiki/October_7_attacks "October 7 attacks") of October 2023.[[167]](#cite_note-167) Israel has the official distinction of being an American [Major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally").
Since 1976, Israel had been the largest annual recipient of U.S. foreign assistance. In 2009, Israel received $2.55 billion in Foreign Military Financing (FMF) grants from the Department of Defense.[[168]](#cite_note-fas2006-10-168) All but 26% of this military aid is for the purchase of military hardware from American companies only.[[168]](#cite_note-fas2006-10-168)

In October 2012, the United States and Israel began their biggest joint air and missile defence exercise, known as [Austere Challenge 12](/wiki/2012_US-Israel_military_exercise "2012 US-Israel military exercise"), involving around 3,500 U.S. troops in the region along with 1,000 IDF personnel.[[169]](#cite_note-169) Germany and Britain also participated.[[170]](#cite_note-170)

Since mid-2017, the United States has operated an anti-missile system in the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") region of Southern Israel, which is manned by 120 US Army personnel. It is a facility used by the U.S. inside a larger [Mashabim](/wiki/Mashabei_Sadeh "Mashabei Sadeh") Israeli Air Force base.[[171]](#cite_note-171)

### India

Further information: [India–Israel relations](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Israel_relations "India–Israel relations")

India and Israel enjoy strong military and strategic ties.[[172]](#cite_note-172) Israeli authorities consider Indian citizens to be the most pro-Israel people in the world.[[173]](#cite_note-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-JINSA01-177) Apart from being Israel's second-largest economic partner in Asia,[[178]](#cite_note-178) India is also the largest customer of Israeli arms in the world.[[179]](#cite_note-auto-179) In 2006, annual military sales between India and Israel stood at US$900 million, totalling around [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")4,200,000,000 (equivalent to $7,636,722,421 in 2025) for the past two decades of arms trade.[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181) Israeli defense firms had the largest exhibition at the 2009 [Aero India](/wiki/Aero_India "Aero India") show, during which Israel offered several state-of-the art weapons to India.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

The first major military deal between the two countries was the sale of Israeli [Phalcon](/wiki/EL/M-2075 "EL/M-2075") [airborne warning and control system](/wiki/Airborne_early_warning_and_control "Airborne early warning and control") (AWACS) radars to the [Indian Air Force](/wiki/Indian_Air_Force "Indian Air Force") in 2004.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) In March 2009, India and Israel signed a US$1.4 billion deal under which Israel would sell India an advanced air-defense system.[[185]](#cite_note-185) India and Israel have also embarked on extensive space cooperation. In 2008, India's [ISRO](/wiki/ISRO "ISRO") launched Israel's most technologically advanced [spy satellite](/wiki/Spy_satellite "Spy satellite") [TecSAR](/wiki/TecSAR "TecSAR").[[186]](#cite_note-jpost-tecsar-186) In 2009, India reportedly developed a high-tech spy satellite [RISAT-2](/wiki/RISAT-2 "RISAT-2") with significant assistance from Israel.[[187]](#cite_note-187) The satellite was successfully launched by India in April 2009.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

According to a [Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times") news story, the [2008 Mumbai attacks](/wiki/2008_Mumbai_attacks "2008 Mumbai attacks") were an attack on the growing India-Israel partnership. It quotes retired Indian [Vice Admiral](/wiki/Vice_admiral_(India) "Vice admiral (India)") Premvir S. Das thus "Their aim was to... tell the Indians clearly that your growing linkage with Israel is not what you should be doing..."[[189]](#cite_note-189) In the past, India and Israel have held numerous joint anti-terror training exercises[[190]](#cite_note-190)

Despite regional crisis and ongoing [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"), defence supply lines to India remained active in 2023-24, as political ties and defence buyers kept the relationship operational.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

### Germany

Further information: [Germany–Israel relations](/wiki/Germany%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Germany–Israel relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/I.n.s._dolfin-03.JPG/250px-I.n.s._dolfin-03.JPG)](/wiki/File:I.n.s._dolfin-03.JPG)

A German-made [Dolphin class submarine](/wiki/Dolphin_class_submarine "Dolphin class submarine")

Germany developed the [Dolphin submarine](/wiki/Dolphin-class_submarine "Dolphin-class submarine") and supplied it to Israel. Two submarines were donated by Germany.[[192]](#cite_note-192) The military co-operation has been discreet but mutually profitable: [Israeli intelligence](/wiki/Israeli_intelligence "Israeli intelligence"), for example, sent captured [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") armor to West Germany to be analyzed. The results aided the German development of an [anti-tank system](/wiki/Anti-tank_warfare "Anti-tank warfare").[[193]](#cite_note-193) Israel also trained members of [GSG 9](/wiki/GSG_9 "GSG 9"), a German counter-terrorism and special operations unit.[[194]](#cite_note-194) The Israeli [Merkava MK IV](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") tank uses a German [V12 engine](/wiki/V12_engine "V12 engine") produced under license.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

In 2008, the website DefenseNews revealed that Germany and Israel had been jointly developing a nuclear warning system, Operation Bluebird.[[196]](#cite_note-israel_germany_nukes1-196)[[197]](#cite_note-israel_germany_nukes2-197)
